title: Released at 104: Lakhan Pasi's case shows us why we need people-centric courts
summary: Taking the needs and priorities of the people as a starting point for the transformation of the courts holds radical potential for the future. What would courts designed keeping people at the centre look like?

title: Listening to the Litigant
summary: What can listening to litigants tell us about how courts can be transformed? A lot. Two stories from litigants about their court experiences, and the questions and insights they leave behind.

title: Treat dispute types as the 'unit of change' to reform our justice system
summary: Treating specific types of disputes as the unit of change can lead to more tailored, effective solutions and improve overall efficiency in handling cases.

title: Listening to the Litigants: Notes from a Field Study
summary: Understanding the experience of the courts from the ordinary litigant's perspective.

title: The unit of change we aim for can make or break reforms
summary: Success in reforms depends on focusing on broader, systemic changes rather than isolated issues, enabling more impactful and sustainable improvements.

title: Transforming Dispute Resolution: Who Holds the Key?
summary: We can no longer imagine standing in a queue for 30 minutes to withdraw money from a bank, but we accept waiting for years to seek justice. Why this should not be the case anymore.

title: Can local innovations solve India's justice problem?
summary: Local innovations, tailored to community needs, can play a crucial role in addressing gaps in India's justice system, offering more accessible and effective solutions at the grassroots level.

title: Justice for all
summary: Local innovations are quietly reshaping India's justice system. Grassroots solutions and collaborative efforts are making justice more accessible and effective.
